questions.prompt: Jordan works 18 hours this week at $17.50/hour. What's Jordan's gross pay, to the nearest dollar?
Jordan's $315 gross pay has $24 withheld for federal tax and $24.10 withheld for FICA (Social Security + Medicare). What's Jordan's net pay, to the nearest dollar?
questions.explanation: $315 − $24 − $24.10 = $266.90, which rounds to $267. FICA applies to nearly every paycheck regardless of income level.

questions.prompt: Jordan wants to budget for next month using this week's numbers. Which figure should Jordan use as the basis for the budget?
options: The $315 gross pay
The $267 net pay
An average of gross and net
questions.explanation: Budgets should always be built on net (take-home) pay — the money that actually lands in the account — not gross pay.

questions.prompt: Using the 50/30/20 rule on a $600 monthly net income, how many dollars should go to needs?
That same month, actual needs come to $360 instead of $300. What's the most financially sound adjustment?
options: Keep wants and savings the same and let the budget go negative
Reduce the wants and/or savings slices to absorb the extra $60 in needs
Just stop tracking the budget since it didn't match the plan
questions.explanation: 50/30/20 is a guideline, not a law — when needs run higher than expected, wants and/or savings should flex down to keep the budget balanced.

questions.prompt: After bumping needs to $360, this person keeps savings fixed at the original $120 (20% of $600). How many dollars are left for wants?
questions.explanation: $600 − $360 (needs) − $120 (savings) = $120 left for wants.

questions.prompt: $500 is deposited in an account earning 5% simple interest per year. How much interest is earned after 1 year?
Instead of simple interest, that $500 earns 5% COMPOUND interest annually. After several years, how will the total compare to simple interest at the same rate?
options: Compound interest earns more over time, since interest starts earning interest too
They earn exactly the same amount always
Simple interest always earns more
questions.explanation: Compound interest grows faster because each year's interest gets added to the balance and starts earning its own interest — the gap widens the longer the money sits.

questions.prompt: Using the Rule of 72, roughly how many years does it take money to double at an 8% annual interest rate? (72 ÷ interest rate)
questions.explanation: 72 ÷ 8 = 9 years — a quick mental-math estimate for doubling time at a given interest rate.

questions.prompt: A credit card balance is $1,000 with a 24% annual interest rate. Roughly how much interest accrues in one month if the balance isn't paid off (24% ÷ 12 months × balance)?
questions.explanation: 24% ÷ 12 = 2% monthly rate. 2% × $1,000 = $20 in interest for that month alone.

questions.prompt: Someone only pays the minimum payment on that $1,000 balance every month instead of paying it off. What's the biggest risk?
options: None — minimum payments are designed to pay off the balance quickly
Interest keeps accruing on the remaining balance, so it can take years to pay off and cost far more than the original $1,000
The credit card company will lower the interest rate automatically
questions.explanation: Minimum payments are often barely more than the interest charged — the payoff can drag on for years and cost multiples of the original balance.

questions.prompt: Which of these is an example of "good debt" vs. "bad debt" thinking?
options: All debt is equally bad and should never be used
Debt used to build long-term value (like a reasonable student loan for a needed degree) is viewed differently than high-interest debt for things that lose value fast
Debt is only bad if the interest rate is above 50%
questions.explanation: The good-debt/bad-debt framework isn't about avoiding all debt — it's about weighing what the debt is funding against its cost.

questions.prompt: A checking account charges a $12 monthly maintenance fee unless a $500 minimum balance is kept. Over a full year of paying that fee, how much is lost to fees alone?
questions.explanation: $12 × 12 months = $144 a year — often avoidable entirely by choosing a no-fee account or maintaining the minimum balance.

questions.prompt: Someone overdrafts their account twice in one month at $35 per overdraft, on top of the $12 monthly fee. What's the total in fees that month?
questions.explanation: $35 + $35 + $12 = $82 in fees in a single month — a strong argument for low-balance alerts and declining overdraft coverage.

questions.prompt: What's the most direct way to avoid both overdraft fees and out-of-network ATM fees?
options: Turn on low-balance alerts, opt out of overdraft coverage, and use only in-network ATMs
Keep as little detail as possible about your balance so you're not stressed
These fees are unavoidable no matter what you do
questions.explanation: Both fee types are almost entirely avoidable with a couple of account settings and paying attention to which ATM you use.

questions.prompt: What's the basic idea behind how insurance works?
options: Everyone gets their exact money back if nothing bad happens
A large pool of people pay smaller regular amounts (premiums) so that when a few of them have a big unexpected loss, the pool covers it
Insurance companies only exist to make claims difficult
questions.explanation: Insurance spreads risk across a large group — most people pay in and never need a big payout, but that's what makes it affordable for the ones who do.

questions.prompt: A car insurance policy has a $500 deductible. After an accident causing $2,000 in damage, how much does the insurance typically cover?
options: The full $2,000, deductible doesn't apply here
$1,500 — the $2,000 damage minus the $500 deductible you pay first
$500 total, that's the deductible
questions.explanation: A deductible is the amount you pay out of pocket before insurance covers the rest: $2,000 − $500 = $1,500 covered by insurance.

questions.prompt: Why do insurance premiums tend to be higher for new teen drivers than experienced adult drivers?
options: Insurance companies just prefer charging teens more for no reason
Statistically, less experienced drivers are involved in more accidents, so insurers price the higher risk into the premium
Teen premiums are actually always lower, not higher
questions.explanation: Premiums are priced based on risk. Less driving experience statistically means higher accident risk, which raises the price of coverage.

questions.prompt: You get a text saying you won a free gift card, but you need to click a link and enter your bank info to "claim" it. What is this?
options: A legitimate reward program
A phishing scam designed to steal your financial information
A standard marketing text you should engage with
questions.explanation: Unsolicited messages asking for financial info in exchange for a "prize" are a classic phishing pattern — real rewards don't require your bank login.

questions.prompt: Someone on social media offers to "flip" your money — send them $100 and get back $500 in a week, guaranteed. What should you do?
options: Send the money, guaranteed returns like this are common and safe
Recognize this as a scam — no legitimate investment guarantees fast, huge, risk-free returns
Send a smaller test amount first to check if it's real
questions.explanation: Guaranteed high returns with no risk is one of the clearest scam signals that exists. Real investing always carries risk, and legitimate returns take time.

questions.prompt: A buyer for something you're reselling online sends you a check for MORE than the price and asks you to refund the difference via gift card. What's happening?
options: A generous but slightly confused buyer
A classic overpayment scam — the check will bounce after you've already sent real gift cards
A normal way people pay online
questions.explanation: The fake check clears temporarily, you send real value back, then the check bounces days later and you're out the money — a very common scam pattern.

questions.prompt: Someone wants to save $900 for a laptop in 15 weeks. How much do they need to save per week?
Three weeks in, they've only managed to save $40/week instead of $60. What are their realistic options?
options: Give up on the goal entirely
Extend the deadline, increase the weekly amount for the remaining weeks, or reduce the goal amount — any of these keeps the goal realistic
Ignore the shortfall and hope it works out
questions.explanation: A goal that's off-track needs one of its three variables adjusted: amount, timeline, or savings rate. Ignoring the gap just guarantees missing the goal.

questions.prompt: They decide to keep the $900 goal but extend the deadline to 22 weeks total. Roughly how much do they need to save per week now, rounded to the nearest dollar?
questions.explanation: $900 ÷ 22 ≈ $40.90, which rounds to $41/week — a much more achievable pace.

questions.prompt: A $60 item is on sale for $45 — but you weren't planning to buy it at all before seeing the sale. Are you "saving" $15?
options: Yes, that's a straightforward $15 in savings
No — if you weren't going to spend the money anyway, spending $45 you wouldn't have otherwise spent isn't savings, it's new spending
It depends on the item's brand
questions.explanation: This is a classic sale trap: you can't "save" money on a purchase you weren't going to make in the first place. That's $45 in new spending, not $15 in savings.

questions.prompt: An item is $50 at one store, or $42 online plus $9 shipping. Which total is cheaper, and by how many dollars?
questions.explanation: Online total: $42 + $9 = $51, which is actually $1 MORE than the in-store $50 — shipping erased the apparent discount.

questions.prompt: Why does the 24-hour rule help with impulse purchases specifically?
options: It guarantees a better price after 24 hours
It creates space between the initial urge and the decision, so you can tell a genuine want from a passing impulse
Stores are legally required to honor a 24-hour cooling-off period
questions.explanation: The rule doesn't change prices — it changes your decision-making by removing the time pressure that drives impulsive buying.

questions.prompt: Which of these is the best use of an emergency fund?
options: A surprise $200 car repair needed to get to work
Buying concert tickets before they sell out
A gaming console you've wanted for months
questions.explanation: Emergency funds are for necessary, unplanned costs — not wants dressed up as urgent.

questions.prompt: Someone has a $300 emergency fund. A surprise repair costs $180. After paying for it from the fund, how much is left in the emergency fund?
After using part of the emergency fund, what's the smartest next move?
options: Leave it depleted since the emergency is over
Prioritize rebuilding the fund back toward its original target before ramping up other savings goals
Close the emergency fund since it clearly "worked" and isn't needed anymore
questions.explanation: An emergency fund is only useful if it's replenished after use — otherwise the next surprise expense has nothing to draw from.
